lines.speaker: NARRATOR
lines.text: The admin corridor does not smell like the ER. That is the first thing you notice. No antiseptic, no oxidized metal, no undertow of sweat and urgency. It smells like carpet and recycled air and the particular silence of people who have decided that paperwork is a kind of power.

lines.speaker: NARRATOR
lines.text: Theo is still in his scrubs when the assistant finds him.

lines.speaker: NARRATOR
lines.text: Her name is Lynne. She has worked this corridor for eleven years. She carries things for Harte the way a bailiff carries a summons — not unkindly, not personally, with the practiced neutrality of someone who has learned that the message and the messenger are not the same thing. She has learned this lesson carefully. She has had to.

lines.speaker: LYNNE
lines.text: Nurse Okafor. Mr. Harte asked me to find you before the shift change.

lines.speaker: NARRATOR
lines.text: She holds out a sealed envelope. His name is printed on the front. Not handwritten. Printed, in the hospital's standard administrative font, as though this is a scheduling notice, a parking update, a reminder about the staff satisfaction survey due next Friday.

lines.speaker: NARRATOR
lines.text: He takes it.

lines.speaker: LYNNE
lines.text: He said to let you know it's not urgent. Just — whenever you have a moment this week.

lines.speaker: NARRATOR
lines.text: She says this the way people say it when the opposite is true. He has heard that register before. He has used it himself, standing beside a bed, telling a family there is time to get coffee, come back, rest — meaning: come back now, there is not much time.

lines.speaker: THEO
lines.text: Thank you, Lynne.

lines.speaker: NARRATOR
lines.text: She walks back toward the frosted glass partition at the corridor's end. He watches her go. Then he looks at the envelope.

lines.speaker: NARRATOR
lines.text: Performance Review — Informal Discussion. The word informal is doing a great deal of work in that phrase. Informal means there is no HR present. Informal means the record of what is said will be Harte's record. Informal means the meeting has not happened yet, which is technically true, and the warning has already arrived, which is also true, and those two facts are not in contradiction.

lines.speaker: NARRATOR
lines.text: He does not open it.

lines.speaker: NARRATOR
lines.text: He puts it in the outer pocket of his scrub top and walks.

lines.speaker: NARRATOR
lines.text: The admin corridor is a hundred and twelve steps from the stairwell door to the exit that opens onto the staff car park. He knows this because he walked it once during a fire drill, counting, because that is a thing his mind does when it is trying not to think. The carpet is grey-blue. The overhead lights are recessed and warmer than the ER's overhead lights, which are white and clinical and make every face look like a diagnosis.

lines.speaker: NARRATOR
lines.text: Here the faces look like meetings.

lines.speaker: NARRATOR
lines.text: A woman from Finance passes him. She nods. He nods. Neither of them speaks. The corridor absorbs them both.

lines.speaker: NARRATOR
lines.text: At the far end, Harte's office door is closed. The light beneath it is on. This means nothing. That light is always on.

lines.speaker: NARRATOR
lines.text: Theo stops walking.

lines.speaker: NARRATOR
lines.text: He stands in the middle of the corridor, sixty-one steps from the stairwell and fifty-one from the exit, and he considers what a performance review means in the specific context of the last six weeks. Harte has watched him document. Harte has watched him take photographs with his phone at the end of shifts, patient initials noted, diversion times noted, bay assignments noted, the small audit trail of a man who cannot yet decide what to do with what he has gathered. Harte is many things. Unobservant is not among them.

lines.speaker: NARRATOR
lines.text: The envelope is light. One sheet, maybe two.

lines.speaker: NARRATOR
lines.text: He thinks about the complaint form in his locker. He thinks about the second patient — the name Agnes gave him, the floor number, the bed. He thinks about the word informal and what it means in a building where Harte has written most of the formal processes himself.

lines.speaker: NARRATOR
lines.text: A porter wheels an empty trolley past the corridor entrance. The squeak of the wheels carries. Then it fades. Then there is only the carpet and the recycled air and the light under the closed door.

lines.speaker: NARRATOR
lines.text: Theo puts his hand against the envelope in his pocket. Feels the edge of it through the fabric.

lines.speaker: NARRATOR
lines.text: He has been in rooms where the danger was visible — chest split open on a gurney, monitor screaming, the gap between what a body can survive and what it is being asked to survive measured in seconds. He knows how to move in those rooms. He knows which number to call, which tray to open, where to stand so he is useful and not in the way.

lines.speaker: NARRATOR
lines.text: This corridor is not that room.

lines.speaker: NARRATOR
lines.text: The danger here has no monitor. It arrived in a sealed envelope with his name in a standard administrative font, carried by a woman who has been careful to separate the message from herself for eleven years, and it is waiting in a meeting that has not started yet, and it was already here, already in his pocket, already pressed against his chest, before he took a single step toward it.

lines.speaker: NARRATOR
lines.text: He turns. Walks back toward the stairwell door. Sixty-one steps. His shift is not done.
